content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


December 16, 2004


Ralph Hicks
Post Office Box 878
Pauma Valley, CA 92061

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-04-237

Dear Mr. Hicks:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your question does not provide a specific situation that could create a conflict of interest, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² In addition, please be aware that the Commission does not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions.

QUESTIONS

1. As an employee of the Port of San Diego, would you be precluded from attempting to sell a product, for the sale of which you will receive a commission, to cities that form the membership of the Port of San Diego, the County of San Diego, or any other city in California?

2. Must you recuse yourself from governmental decisions in which you are involved as a Port of San Diego employee regarding the City of Chula Vista, a member city of the Port of San Diego, if you receive

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).

File No. 1-04-237
Page No. 2

commission income from the sale of the product to the City of Chula
Vista?

3. Would you have any conflicts of interest regarding insurance carriers or
developers not doing business with the Port of San Diego?

CONCLUSION

1. Nothing in the Act prohibits you from pursuing outside employment,
including acting as a sales representative for, and receiving commission
income from, a privately owned company. Although there may be
occasions when your activities are limited by conflict-of-interest rules
discussed below, nothing in the Act or the conflict-of-interest rules
prevents you from holding a second position, whether it is in public or
private employment. We strongly suggest, however, that you consult the
Port of San Diego's statement of incompatible activities for guidelines
when considering receiving outside income concurrent with your position
with the Port.³

2. and 3. Your ability to make governmental decisions will depend on
applying the appropriate materiality standards and verifying that there will
be no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any persons who
are a source of income to you. The "public generally" exception may
apply to conflicts of interest arising by virtue of your possible income
from the City of Chula Vista or other governmental entities. The
exception will apply if the effect of a decision flows to all residents in the
jurisdiction. Under such circumstances, the effect of the decision would
be the same as the effect on the public generally. If, on the other hand, the
effect of the decision does not flow to City of Chula Vista residents, then
the "public generally" exception will not apply. In addition, if you are
disqualified on the basis of effects on your personal finances, the "public
generally" exception will most likely not apply.

FACTS

You are the director of land use planning for the Port of San Diego ("Port"), a
trustee of state tidelands surrounding San Diego Bay. The Port has five member
cities: San Diego, Chula Vista, National City, Coronado and Imperial Beach. The
member cities appoint commissioners who make up the port's policy board. The
individual member cities do not, however, control or operate the port.

³ We also strongly encourage you to consider whether Government Code section 1090, the law
prohibiting public officials from having an interest in contracts with their own agencies, may also be
implicated by your situation. The Commission is charged with interpreting and enforcing the provisions of
the Act, and may provide advice only with respect to those provisions (section 83114), so we refer you to
the Attorney General's office for questions regarding Government Code section 1090.

File No. I-04-237
Page No. 3

You are currently the lead staff person working on planning the Chula Vista
waterfront. In this capacity, you recommend issues related to land use, consultant
selection and regulatory matters to the Chula Vista City Council and the Port
commissioners.

In a private capacity, you represent Fire Scout Company, a company located in
Boulder, Colorado engaged in making wildfire detection sensors which can detect a small
fire and alert emergency personnel before the fire gets out of control. You are neither an
employee of the company nor do you have an ownership interest, but are, instead,
involved in the sale of the wildfire detection sensors. Rather, you receive commission
income when you successfully convince a buyer to purchase the wildfire detection sensor
from the company. Because Chula Vista was affected directly by last October's
wildfires, the city has expressed an interest in the regional wildfire detection system at a
cost of approximately $150,000. The contract would be signed by the Fire Scout
Company and you would receive a commission from the sale.

With the likelihood that insurance companies may provide discounts based on the
wildfire detection sensor, you anticipate that other governmental agencies, including the
County of San Diego and various other cities in California, as well as private residential
developers will have an interest in this technology and you would like to offer the
regional wildfire detection system to those prospective purchasers as well. You have
stated that you will not be attempting to sell the wildfire detection sensor to the Port.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest rules prohibit a public official from making,
participating in making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a
governmental decision in which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she
has a "financial interest." (Section 87100.) Section 87103 provides that a public official
has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family" or on any of
the official's economic interests. Nothing in the Act prohibits you from seeking and
obtaining outside employment. To determine whether a public official has such a
"conflict of interest" in a governmental decision, the FPPC has developed a standard,
eight-step analysis outlined at subdivisions 1 through 8 of regulation 18700(b). A review
of this standardized analysis will assist in determining when, if at all, you may find
yourself faced with a conflict of interest.

Step 1. Are you a public official?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to public officials, defined as
"every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency."

File No. 1-04-237
Page No. 4

(Section 82048.) As the director of land use planning for the Port of San Diego, a local
government agency, you are a public official.

**Step 2: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?**

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public official is
"making, participating in making, or using or attempting to use his/her official position to
influence a governmental decision" in which he/she knows or has reason to know he/she
has a financial interest. (Section 87100; regulation 18700(b)(2).)

Making a governmental decision entails such things as voting on a matter,
appointing a person, obligating or committing an agency to a course of action, or
determining not to act because of a conflict of interest. (Regulation 18702.1.)
Participating in a governmental decision is a broad category and includes advising or
making recommendations to the decision-maker or exercising judgment for the purpose
of influencing a decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use
his or her official position to influence a decision before his or her own agency if, for the
purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts or appears before any member,
officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Section 87100; regulation
18702.3(a).) There is another rule regarding influencing an agency that applies when a
governmental decision is within or before an agency other than the official's. In that
context, "the official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts or purports to act
on behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her agency to any member, officer,
employee or consultant of an agency." (Regulation 18702.3(b).)

As lead planner making recommendations to the City of Chula Vista Council and
the Port Commissioners in decisions regarding the Chula Vista Waterfront, you will be
involved in participating in and influencing governmental decisions regarding the
waterfront project. There may be other instances when you are involved in actually
making governmental decisions.

When you act in your private capacity and not within the authority of your
position as an employee of the Port, you will not be "making" or "participating in
making" a governmental decision when engaged in attempting to sell the wildlife sensor
to the City of Chula Vista. However, the fact that you intend to negotiate a sale with the
City of Chula Vista raises issues concerning "influencing" a governmental decision.

There are two rules used to determine whether a public official is using or
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision. The
first rule applies when the relevant governmental decision is within or before the public

File No. 1-04-237
Page No. 5

official's own agency,⁴ or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of
the public official's agency. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) In that case,

". . . the official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence
the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official
contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to
influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the
official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer." (Regulation
18702.3(a).)

The second rule applies when the relevant governmental decision is within or
before an agency not covered by subdivision (a) of regulation 18702.3. Because the City
of Chula Vista is not under the budgetary control of your agency, the second rule applies.
Under this rule:

"...the official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence
the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts
or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her
agency's to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an agency."
(Regulation 18702.3(b).)

Step 3. What are your economic interests?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from
economic interests. The economic interests from which conflicts may arise are defined
within section 87103 of the Act and regulations 18703 - 18703.5. The economic
interest implicated by your request for advice is:

Any source of income to you, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.)

Clearly, the Fire Scout Company will be an economic interest of yours if you
receive commission payment from them. However, your question is not whether you
may be required to disqualify yourself where the Fire Scout Company appears before
you, but rather will you be required to disqualify yourself when those persons⁵ to whom
you have sold the wildfire detection sensor are the subject of decisions in which you

⁴ With respect to regulation 18702.3, the Commission will generally treat each public body as
separate public agencies. (Hons Advice Letter, No. I-00-255; Larmore Advice Letter, No. A-00-275; Stout
Advice Letter, No. I-88-313.

⁵ "Person" is defined in the Act as: "an individual, proprietorship, firm, partnership, joint venture,
syndicate, business trust, company, corporation, limited liability company, association, committee, and any
other organization or group of persons acting in concert." (Section 82047.)

File No. 1-04-237
Page No. 6

will be involved. Thus, the next step is to discover whether or not those who purchase
the products as a result of your sales are sources of income to you. For the answer we
turn to regulation 18703.3 which says that, for purposes of disqualification under
sections 87100 and 87103, a public official has an economic interest in any person from
whom he or she has received income, including commission income. Regulation
18703.3(c)(2)(F) further establishes that in the case of a retail or wholesale salesperson,
the sources of income in a specific sale or similar transaction include:

"(i) The person, store or other business entity which provides the
salesperson with the product or service to sell and for which the salesperson
acts as a representative in the transaction; and

(ii) The person who purchases the product or service."

Regulation 18703.3(c)(3) further establishes that, for purposes of determining
whether disqualification is required, "the full gross value of any commission income for
a specific sale or similar transaction shall be attributed to each source of income in that
sale or transaction."

Thus, in a given sales transaction in which you receive commission income, both
the person purchasing the product and Fire Scout Company will be economic interests.

In addition to the economic interest explored in the rest of this letter, please note
that you have an economic interest in your personal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities, as well as those of your immediate family. This is known as the "personal
financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.5.) This economic interest
would be particularly relevant if, say, you were involved in your governmental capacity
in decisions regarding whether or not a particular governmental entity would purchase the
wildfire detection sensor.

Step 4. Will these economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?

The next step is to determine whether your economic interests will be involved
directly or indirectly in the decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(4).) A person is directly
involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by
an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject
of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,

File No. I-04-237
Page No. 7

denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person." (Regulation 18704.1(a).)

Thus, the question which determines whether a source of income is directly or
indirectly involved is whether the source of income is a named party in, or the initiator or
subject of, the proceedings in which these decisions will occur. A person who is a source
of income is deemed to be indirectly involved if they do not fall into one of the preceding
categories. (Regulation 18704.1(a).) In the absence of a specific governmental decision,
we can only assume that, in matters involving Chula Vista Waterfront, the City of Chula
Vista, a potential customer for the wildfire detection system, has initiated the proceeding
to develop the waterfront or is, at least, the subject of the proceeding to develop the
waterfront. Regarding other potential sources of commission income to you, such as the
private residential developers and insurance carriers not doing business with the Port to
whom you refer in your letter, if a person who is an economic interest to the public
official is not directly involved in a governmental decision, the person is deemed to be
indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)

With respect to personal finances, regulation 18704.5 provides that a public
official or his or her immediate family is deemed to be directly involved in a
governmental decision which has any financial effect on his or her personal finances or
those of his or her immediate family.

Steps 5-6. What are the applicable materiality standards and is it
reasonably foreseeable that the financial effects of the governmental
decisions upon these officials' economic interests will meet this materiality
standard?

Once you identify your relevant economic interests, you must evaluate whether it
is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a material financial effect on any of
those economic interests by ascertaining the applicable materiality standard (regulations
18700(b)(5), 18705 - 18705.5) and then determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable
that the standard will be met.⁶ (Regulations 18700(b)(6) and 18706.) Because only one
potential decision regarding the Chula Vista Waterfront is identified in your letter, we
will focus the rest of the analysis on the City of Chula Vista as the pertinent source of
income. Enclosed, however, is the fact sheet "Can I Vote?" so that, in the future, you can
perform the analysis with other sources of income that may be affected by decisions you
make or participate in making as an employee of the Port of San Diego.

Regulation 18705.3 provides that any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a
source of income, whether the source is a governmental entity, an individual or a business

⁶ An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially likely."
(Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial consequences of a
governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made depends on the specific facts
surrounding the decision. A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable.
On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.

File No. I-04-237
Page No. 8

entity, is deemed to be material and thus disqualifying when the source of income is
"directly involved" in a governmental decision made by a public official. As stated
above, it appears that the City of Chula Vista is very possibly directly involved in
decisions regarding the Chula Vista Waterfront. If, however, the City of Chula Vista, or
any other governmental entity, is indirectly involved in a decision, you will use the
materiality thresholds for nonprofits which includes governmental entities, who are
sources of income found at regulation 18705.3(b)(2) (copy enclosed).

In addition to governmental entities as sources of commission income, you also
ask about potential conflict-of-interest issues regarding private residential developers and
insurance carriers not doing business with the Port of San Diego. As stated above in Step
4 of the analysis, if a person who is an economic interest to the public official is not
directly involved in a governmental decision, the person is deemed to be indirectly
involved. (Regulation 18704.1(b).) The materiality standard applicable to a business
entity that is indirectly involved in a governmental decision is set forth in regulation
18705.3(c), which lists thresholds for business entities that fall within a gross revenue
category or meet the requirements to be listed on the New York Stock Exchange,
NASDAQ or American Stock Exchange. If these requirements do not apply, a third
category states:

"(4) . . .the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business
entity is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:

(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000
or more; or,

(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,

(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."

(Regulation 18705.1(c)(4)(A)-(C).)

Because you do not provide any facts relevant to the size of the private residential
developers and insurance carriers who may be potential sources of commission income to
you nor do we have facts regarding any decision in which you could be involved
regarding such entities, you would have to determine if there is a material financial effect
on the business on a case-by-case basis. We have enclosed a copy of regulation 18705.1
for your assistance and encourage you to write as specific facts and decisions develop.

File No. I-04-237
Page No. 9

Steps 7. & 8. "Public Generally" and "Legally Required Participation"
Exceptions

Step 7 is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and material
financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally. (Section 83112; regulation 18707.) Under the "public generally"
exception, an official with a conflict of interest may still participate in a decision if the
financial effect of the decision on the official's economic interest is indistinguishable
from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; regulation 18707(a).) In this
analysis of the "public generally" exception as it applies to your situation, we will
concentrate on the very real issues of your potentially disqualifying income from the City
of Chula Vista.

Regulation 18707.1(b) sets out specific standards for this exception by providing
in pertinent part:

"(1) Significant Segment. The governmental decision will affect a
'significant segment' of the public generally if any of the following are
affected as set forth below:

¶. . .¶

"(D) Governmental Entities. For decisions that affect a federal, state or
local government entity in which the public official has an economic
interest, the decision will affect all members of the public under the
jurisdiction of that governmental entity.

¶. . .¶

"(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The governmental decision will
financially affect a public official's economic interest in substantially the
same manner as it will affect the significant segment identified in
subdivision (b)(1) of this regulation."

The comment to regulation 18707.1 establishes that "[t]he term 'affect all
members of the public' is intended to cover decisions affecting the public in general but
to exclude decisions which uniquely benefit a public official." With respect to decisions
involving the City of Chula Vista, if the effects will flow to the public generally you will
be able to participate. However, if the basis for your disqualification results from an
effect on your personal finances, this exception would most likely not serve to allow your
participation.

The last step of the conflict-of-interest analysis explores whether or not the public
official is legally required to make or participate in the making of a governmental
decision so that disqualification is not required. (Regulation 18708.) Nothing in the facts

File No. I-04-237
Page No. 10

that you have provided suggests that this exception is as of yet applicable to your
situation.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Adrianne Korchmaros
Political Reform Consultant II

Enclosures

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict-of-interest rules prohibit a public official from making,
participating in making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a
govemmental decision in which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she
has a "financial interest." (Section 87100.) Section 87103 provides that a public official
has a "financial interest" in agovernmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family'' or on any of
the official's economic interests. Nothing in the Act prohibits you from seeking and
obtaining outside ernployment. To determine whether a public official has such a
"conflict of interest" in a governmental decision, the FPPC has developed a standard,
eight-step analysis outlined at subdivisions I through 8 of regulation 18700(b). A review
of this standardized analysis will assist in determining when, if at all, you may find
yourself faced with a conflict of interest.
Step 1. Are you a public official?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to public officials, defined as
"every member, officer, ernployee or consultant of a state or local government agency."
(Section 82048.) As the director of land use planning for the Port of San Diego, a local
government agency, you are a public official.
Step 2: Are you making participating in making, or influencing a
goYernmental decision?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public official is
"making, participating in making, or using or attempting to use his/her official position to
influence a govemmental decision" in which helshe knows or has reason to know helshe
has a financial interest. (Section 87100; regulation 18700(bX2).)
Making a govemment decision entails such things as voting on a matter,
appointing a person, obligating or committing an agency to a course of action, or
determining not to act because of a conflict of interest. (Regulation 18702.1.)
Participating in a governmental decision is a broad category and includes advising or
making reconlmendations to the decision-maker or exercising judgment for the purpose
of influencing a decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use
his or her official position to influence a decision before his or her own agency if, for the
pu{pose of influencing the decision, the official contacts or appears before any member,
officer, ernployee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Section 87100; regulation
18702.3(a).) There is another rule regarding influencing an agency that applies when a
govemmental decision is within or before an agency other than the official's. In that
context, "the official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts or purports to act
on behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her agency to any member, officer,
employee or consultant of an agency." (Regulation 18702.3(b).)
As lead planner making recommendations to the City of Chula Vista Council and
the Port Commissioners in decisions regarding the Chula Vista Waterfront, you will be
involved in participating in and influencing governmental decisions regarding the
waterfront project. There may be other instances when you are involved in actually
making govemmental decisions.
When you act in yourprivate capacity and not within the authority of your
position as an employee of the Port, you will not be'tnaking" or "participating in
making" a govemmental decision when engaged in attonpting to sell the wildfire sensor
to the City of Chula Vista. However, the fact that you intend to negotiate a sale with the
City of Chula Vista raises issues concerning "influencing" a governmental decision.
There are two rules used to determine whether a public official is using or
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision. The
first rule applies when the relevant govemmental decision is within or before the public
official's own agency,4 or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of
the public official's agency. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) In that case,
oo. . . the official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence
the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official
contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Atternpts to
influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the
official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer." (Regulation
18702.3(a).)
The second rule applies when the relevant govemmental decision is within or
before an agency not covered by subdivision (a) of regulation 18702.3. Because the City

Underthis rule:
"...the official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence
the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts
or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her
agency to any member, officer, ernployee or consultant of an agency."
(Regulation I 8702.3(b).)
Step 3. \Mhat are your economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from
economic interests. The economic interests from which conflicts may arise are defined
within section 87103 of the Act and regulations 18703 - 18703.5. The economic
interest implicated by your request for advice is:
Any source of income to you, including promised incomg which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 871 03(c); regulation 18703.3.)
Clearly, the Fire Scout Company will be an economic interest of yours if you
receive commission payment from them. However, your question is not whether you
maybe required to disqualiff yourself where the Fire Scout Company appears before
you, but rather will you be required to disqualiff yourself when those personss to whom
you have sold the wildfire detection sensor are the subject of decisions in which you
4 With respect to regulation l87}2.3,the Commission will generally heat eachpublic body as
separate public agencies. (Hons Adice Lrtter, No. I-00-255; Larmore Adice Letter, No . A-00-275; Stout
Advice Letter, No. I-88-313.
5 '?erson" is defined in the Act as: "an individual, proprietorship, firm, par0rership, joint venture,
syndicate, business trust, company, corporation, limited liability company, association, committee, and any
other organization or group of persons acting in concert." (Section 82047.)
will be involved. Thus, the next step is to discover whether or not those who purchase
the products as a result of your sales are sources of income to you. For the answer we
turn to regulation 18703.3 which says that, for purposes of disqualification under
sections 871 00 and 87 703 , a public official has an economic interest in any person from
whom he or she has received income, including commission income. Regulation
18703.3(c)(2XF) further establishes that in the case of a retail or wholesale salesperson,
the sources of income in a specific sale or similar transaction include:
"(i) The person, store or other business antity which provides the
salesperson with the product or service to sell and for which the salesperson
acts as a representative in the transaction; and
(ii) The person who purchases the product or service."
Regulation 18703.3(c)(3) further establishes that, for purposes of determining
whether disqualification is required, "the full gross value of any commission income for
a specific sale or similar transaction shall be attributed to each source of income in that
sale or transaction."
Thus, in a given sales transacfion in which you receive commission income, both
the person purchasing the product and Fire Scout Company will be economic interests.
In addition to the economic interest explored in the rest of this letter, please note
that you have an economic interest in your personal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities, as well as those of your immediate family. This is known as the'lersonal
financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.5.) This economic interest
would be particularly relevant if say, you were involved in your governmental capacity
in decisions regarding whether or not a particular govemmental entity would purchase the
wildfire detection sensor.
Step 4. WiII these economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
The next step is to determine whether your economic interests will be involved
directly or indirectly in the decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(4).) A person is directly
involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by
an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject
of aproceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person." (Regulation 18704.1(a).)
Thus, the question which determines whether a source of income is directly or
indirectly involved is whether the source of income is a named party in, or the initiator or
subject of, the proceedings in which these decisions will occur. A person who is a source
of income is deerned to be indirectly involved if they do not fall into one of the preceding
categories. (Regulation 18704.1(a).) In the absence of a specific governmental decision,
we can only assume that, in matters involving Chula Vista Waterfront, the City of Chula
Vista, a potential customer for the wildfire detection system, has initiated the proceeding
to develop the waterfront or is, at least, the subject of the proceeding to develop the
waterfront. Regarding other potential sources of commission income to you, such as the
private residential developers and insurance carriers not doing business with the Port to
whom you refer in your letter, if a person who is an economic interest to the public
official is not directly involved in a govemmental decision, the person is deemed to be
indirectly involved. (Regulation 1 870a. 1@).)
With respect to personal finances, regulation 18704.5 provides that apublic
official or his or her immediate family is deemed to be directly involved in a
govemmental decision which has any financial effect on his or her personal finances or
those ofhis or her immediate family.
Steps 5-6. What are the applicable materiality standards and is it
reasonably foreseeable that the financial effects of the governmental
decisions upon these officials' economic interests will meet this materiality
standard?
Once you identiff your relevant economic interests, you must evaluate whether it
is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a material financial effect on any of
those economic interests by ascertaining the applicable materiality standmd (regulations
18700(bX5), 18705 - 18705.5-) and then determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable
that the standard will be met.6 (Regulations 1S700(b)(6) and 18706.) Because only one
potential decision regarding the Chula Vista Waterfront is identified in your letter, we
will focus the rest of the analysis on the City of Chula Vista as the pertinent source of
income. Enclosed, however, is the fact sheet "Can I Vote?" so that, in the future, you can
perform the analysis with other sources of income that may be affected by decisions you
make orparticipate in making as an employee of the Port of San Diego.
Regulation 18705.3 provides that any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a
source of income, whether the source is a governmental entity, an individual or a business
6 An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" ifthe effect is "substantially likely."
(Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial consequences of a
govemmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made depends on the specific facts
surrounding the decision. A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable.
On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
entity, is deemed to be material and thus disquali$ring when the source of income is
"directly involved" in a governmental decision made by a public official. As stated
above, it appears that the City of Chula Vista is very possibly directly involved in
decisions regardingthe Chula Vista Waterfront. If, however, the City of Chula Vista, or
any other govemmental entity is indirectly involved in a decision, you will use the
materiality thresholds for nonprofits which includes governmental entities, who are
sources of income found at regulation 18705.3(b)(2) (copy anclosed).
In addition to governmental entities as sources of commission income, you also
ask about potential conflict-of-interest issues regarding private residential developers and
insurance camiers not doing business with the Port of San Diego. As stated above in Step
4 of the analysis, if a person who is an economic interest to the public official is not
directly involved in a govemmental decision, the person is deemed to be indirectly
involved. (Regulation 18704.1(b).) The materiality standard applicable to a business
entity that is indirectly involved in a governmental decision is set forth in regulation
18705.3(c), which lists thresholds for business entities that fall within a gross revenue
category or meet the requirements to be listed on the New York Stock Exchange,
NASDAQ or American Stock Exchange. If these requirements do not apply, a third
category states:
*(4) . ..the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business
entity is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000
or more; or,
(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."
(Regulation I 8705. I (c)(4XA)-(C).)
Because you do not provide any facts relevant to the size of the private residential
developers and insurance carriers who may be potential sources of commission income to
you nor do we have facts regarding any decision in which you could be involved
regarding such entities, you would have to determine if there is a material financial effect
on the business on a case-by-case basis. We have enclosed a copy of regulation 18705.1
for your assistance and encourage you to write as specific facts and decisions develop.
Steps 7. & 8. "Public Generally" and'6l,egally Required Participation,,
Exceptions
Step 7 is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and material
financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally. (Section 83112; regulation 18707.) Under the "public generally''
exception, an of,Ecial with a conflict of interest may still participate in a decision if the
financial ef;lect of the decision on the official's economic interest is indistinguishable
from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87l}3;regulation 18707(a).) In this
analysis of the "public generally" exception as it applies to your situation, we will
concentrate on the very real issues of yourpotentially disqualifying income from the City
of Chula Vista.
Regulation 18707.1(b) sets out specific standards for this exception by providing
in pertinent part:
"(1) Significant Segment. The governmental decision will affect a
'significant segment' of the public generally if any of the following are
affected as set forth below:
tL . .'11
"(D) Governmental Entities. For decisions that affect a federal, state or
local govemment entity in which the public official has an economic
interest, the decision will affect all members of the public under the
jurisdiction of that governmental entity.
fl. . .11
"(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The governmental decision will
financially affect a public official's economic interest in substantially the
same manner as it will affect the significant segment identified in
subdivision (bXl) of this regulation."
The comment to regulation 18707.1establishes that "[t]he term 'affect all
mernbers of the public' is intended to cover decisions affecting the public in general but
to exclude decisions which uniquely benefit a public official." With respect to decisions
involving the City of Chula Vista, if the effects will flow to the public generally you will
be able to participate. However, if the basis for your disqualification results from an
effect on your personal finances, this exception would most likely not serve to allow your
participation.
The last step of the conflict-of-interest analysis explores whether or not the public
official is legally required to make or participate in the making of a governmental
decision so that disqualification is not required. (Regulation 18708.) Nothing in the facts


PageNo. l0
that you have provided suggests that this exception is as of yet applicable to your
situation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: As an employee of the Port of San Diego, would you be precluded
from attemrpting to sell a product, for the sale of which you will
receive a commission, to cities that form the membership of the port of
San Diego, the County of San Diego, or any other city in California?
Must you recuse yourself from govemmental decisions in which you
are involved as a Port of San Diego employee regarding the City of
Chula Vista, a member city of the Port of San Diego, if you receive
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83 I 14; regulation I 8329(c)(3), copy enclosed).
1
2
commission income from the sale of the product to the City of Chula
Vista?
Would you have any conflicts of interest regarding insurance earriers or
developers not doing business with the Port of San Diego?

CONCLUSION: 1. Nothing in the Act prohibits you from pursuing outside employment,
including acting as a sales representative for, and receiving commission
income from, apivately owned company. Although there may be
occasions when your activities are limited by conflict-of-interest rules
discussed below, nothing in the Act or the conflict-of-interest rules
prevents you from holding a second position, whether it is in public or
private ernployment. We strongly suggest, however, that you consult the
Port of San Diego's statement of incompatible activities for guidelines
when considering receiving outside income concurrent with your position
with the Port.3
2. and 3. Your ability to make govemmental decisions will depend on
applyng the appropriate materiality standards and verirying that there will
be no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any persons who
are a source of income to you. The "public generally''exception may
apply to conflicts of interest arising by virtue of your possible income
from the City of Chula Vista or other governmental entities. The
exception will apply if the effect of a decision flows to all residents in the
jurisdiction. Under such circumstances, the effect ofthe decision would
be the same as the effect on the public generally. If, on the other hand, the
effect of the decision does not flow to City of Chula Vista residents, then
the'lublic ge,nerally''exception will not apply. In addition, if you are
disqualified on the basis of efi[ects on yolr personal finances, the "public
generally''exception will most likely not apply.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 December 16, 2004 Ralph Hicks Post Office Box 878 Pauma Valley, CA 92061 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-04-237 Dear Mr. Hicks: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your question does not provide a specific situation that could create a conflict of interest, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² In addition, please be aware that the Commission does not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions. QUESTIONS 1. As an employee of the Port of San Diego, would you be precluded from attempting to sell a product, for the sale of which you will receive a commission, to cities that form the membership of the Port of San Diego, the County of San Diego, or any other city in California? 2. Must you recuse yourself from governmental decisions in which you are involved as a Port of San Diego employee regarding the City of Chula Vista, a member city of the Port of San Diego, if you receive ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed). File No. 1-04-237 Page No. 2 commission income from the sale of the product to the City of Chula Vista? 3. Would you have any conflicts of interest regarding insurance carriers or developers not doing business with the Port of San Diego? CONCLUSION 1. Nothing in the Act prohibits you from pursuing outside employment, including acting as a sales representative for, and receiving commission income from, a privately owned company. Although there may be occasions when your activities are limited by conflict-of-interest rules discussed below, nothing in the Act or the conflict-of-interest rules prevents you from holding a second position, whether it is in public or private employment. We strongly suggest, however, that you consult the Port of San Diego's statement of incompatible activities for guidelines when considering receiving outside income concurrent with your position with the Port.³ 2. and 3. Your ability to make governmental decisions will depend on applying the appropriate materiality standards and verifying that there will be no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any persons who are a source of income to you. The "public generally" exception may apply to conflicts of interest arising by virtue of your possible income from the City of Chula Vista or other governmental entities. The exception will apply if the effect of a decision flows to all residents in the jurisdiction. Under
